critic_raw: The design handles most concerns well. Let me raise two harder issues. First: the baseline feature set includes 'agent prior escalation rate (aggregate statistics, not identity).' This means the baseline has information about how frequently the handling agent escalates tickets — a strong behavioral predictor. The transformer does not have this feature (it uses the same features plus sequence). Is the comparison fair if the baseline already has a strong behavioral predictor that may explain most of the variance? Second: the test set covers months 31-36 while the validation set is months 25-30. What if there is a systematic change in escalation policy between months 30 and 31 that creates a distribution shift? The model would be evaluated on data from after this shift without any indication from the validation performance.
defender_raw: Round 1: On agent escalation rate in baseline: both models receive identical features — the design explicitly states 'Two feature sets are constructed: Baseline features: [list including agent prior escalation rate]' and 'Transformer features: All baseline features plus the full chronological sequence.' The transformer receives all baseline features INCLUDING agent escalation rate, plus the sequence. The comparison tests whether the additional sequence information adds value over the full set of metadata and BoW features (which already includes agent escalation rate). This is a fair incremental test. On policy shift between months 30-31: the temporal split is designed precisely to detect whether test performance diverges from validation performance. A sudden policy shift would show as a drop in test PR-AUC vs. validation PR-AUC — this is informative and honest. Both models face the same shift, preserving comparative validity.

rounds.critic_followup: On the transformer having all baseline features plus sequence — confirmed, this is in the design. Then the question is: if agent escalation rate is a very strong predictor, and both models have it, the marginal contribution of sequence modeling to PR-AUC improvement may be small. This is not a flaw but a potentially false negative — the experiment may not be powered to detect a genuine sequence modeling advantage if the dominant predictor (agent escalation rate) already saturates much of the variance.
rounds.defender_followup: You've correctly identified a statistical power consideration. If agent escalation rate explains most variance, the marginal PR-AUC gain from sequence modeling will be small and may not reach statistical significance in bootstrap CIs. However, this is a legitimate experimental outcome — if metadata dominates, that is the correct finding. The design is not powered to guarantee detection of a specific effect size; it reports what is observed. This is an honest evaluation, not a flaw in the design. The secondary metrics (precision at fixed recall thresholds) may also reveal improvements in specific operating regimes even if overall PR-AUC is similar.

rounds.critic_followup: Acknowledged on statistical power. On the policy shift concern: if both models face the same shift and PR-AUC drops for both, how would we distinguish 'the transformer failed to generalize' from 'the policy changed and no model can generalize'? Is there a design element to make this distinction?
rounds.defender_followup: The product-line stratified analysis provides partial disambiguation — if PR-AUC drops uniformly across all product lines in months 31-36, this suggests a global policy change rather than model-specific failure. If it drops for certain lines and not others, model-specific generalization failure is indicated. The design's temporal split also naturally establishes a validation vs. test PR-AUC comparison — a small test vs. validation gap suggests robust generalization; a large gap suggests distribution shift. These diagnostics are sufficient to characterize the source of any performance drop.

adjudication_raw: Two rounds addressing: (1) agent escalation rate as dominant predictor reducing statistical power, (2) policy shift ambiguity between validation and test periods. Both resolved with sound arguments: low statistical power is a valid null result; product-line stratification and validation/test gap comparison are sufficient diagnostics. Verdict: defense_wins.
